title: Age and Maturity
wikitext:
[]: Age and Maturity             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Mon Jun 08, 2009 6:48 pm It's pretty clear that the children in NGE aren't anything close to normal fourteen year olds. Their bodies, thoughts, actions, and emotions aren't at all representative of someone as young as they are. The pilot cast could've been replaced by twenty year olds and it would have felt much more fitting, IMO. The example that stands out most in my mind is one from the manga. When Rei has her visitation with the halo/tentacle angel, it accuses her of having an ugly heart because she wanted Shinji all to herself and was jealous of the way he visited the comatose Asuka everyday. It's a moving scene, but it only works if Rei is emotionally mature. Assuming she's a normal fourteen year old, she has what we called a CRUSH. It's completely harmless and completely normal. But knowing that she (her body) is even younger than that, and knowing that she barely even understands what emotions ARE, there's no way you can hold her accountable for her feelings. Another example is how the children act and are treated with respect to sex. Asuka is all over Kaji wanting "kissing and beyond," and I strongly doubt she would have anything more than an extremely elementary understanding of what that would even entail. Kaji responds correctly, but even so, fourteen year olds that I've known aren't like that at all. Also, consider Misato and Shinji. She tries to encourage him with sex ("This is the best I can do for you right now" "We'll do the rest when you get back"). Granted, that may be Misato's standard way of dealing with such problems, but even she should realize that that's just not applicable to someone as young as Shinji. And we can't forget all the existential stuff that drives NGE. That sort of thing is way beyond the capacity or even the interest of a fourteen year old. All the "what is the meaning of my existance?" "what is my purpose?" "what is my influence on and affliation with others?" junk wouldn't even begin to occur to someone that young, at least not in such concrete terms. Yes, someone like Shinji who just wants the love (or simply the recognition) of his father will be hurting and will have feelings related to the situation. But there's no way they'd extrapolate and specify it to the extent that occurs in NGE. So given all that, why would characters with this level of maturity be represented by kids as young as these? Is it because that fits within the time span between 2I and present day? Is it because the whole mother/child connection works better with younger people? Is it something else? Or am I just overlooking the fact that exceptional childhoods generate exceptional characters?

[]: Re: Age and Maturity             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Mon Jun 08, 2009 6:55 pm Gamer_2k4 wrote: It's pretty clear that the children in NGE aren't anything close to normal fourteen year olds.

[]:             Quote Post by BattleMonkey » Mon Jun 08, 2009 7:02 pm No matter how mature they might seem in some sequences, the kids still act like little kids. Only Rei comes off as being truly mature just because of her stoic nature. Rei as a whole is a hard thing to really analyze considering she also contains the oldest soul in the world technically, as well as all the other influences being placed on her. Asuka is probably one of the least mature acting of all the characters, she may have a smart exterior or facade around her, but as soon as her mouth opens she shows her true age. Asuka pushing forth the idea of having sex does not in any way make a person mature, in reality the improper use of sex just goes to show how immature so many kids are when they ruin their lives by jumping into it. To have thoughts of a mature level means nothing about someones age, if they do not truly understand it. Shinji's thoughts might sound "smart" and of someone older, but his lack of comprehension on so many of these matters just goes to show that he truly is a kid. All Shinji does is question, yet he never understands, even when the answer is drilled into him several times over and over again in the series. My little niece asked me what the meaning of life was... did that make her really mature? If I even had an answer for her, would it have truly been understood? Being thoughtful is a good thing, but if you lack the ability to ever actually take anything from your experiences, your not showing any growth. Shinji just ran away from the answers. At the same time I don't think you give proper credit to how mature a 14 year old can truly be. They are the age of transition, this is normal for them to be developing all kinds of new feelings and emotions. The angst so common with teens often begins around this time because of all the new feelings they are developing as they are leaving behind their child like ways, this transitional period is a very emotional moment in their lives. The kids in Eva not only are at this precious age, but they have not normal backgrounds, and are forced into life and death situations of conflict generally not seen by people till at least their later teens. It all has it's toll on a person.

[]:             Quote Post by oOoOoOo » Mon Jun 08, 2009 7:25 pm When I was fourteen I would have called those Eva pilots "squares". I definitely knew what to do with all three holes by Asuka's age. Personally, I see little connection between age and maturity, once you've hit adolescence. Experience doesn't equal maturity, either. Rather, it is if you learn from your experience. In that sense, it is all about intelligence. The stupidest adult will always be less mature than the smartest child. (Although the adult may be able to "act" better than the child, this doesn't mean the inside of their head isn't childlike.) When a stupid child throws a tantrum, after all, nobody gets shot in the face. The only difference between the teachers' lounge and the schoolyard is that the schoolyard doesn't have liquor in it. I know plenty of middle-age adults who have the critical thinking capacity of a dirty syringe. Besides, we're all just animals with pantsu and cell phones. It is a child who says, "I'm not a child" and it is an adult who says "I am a child". Put it this way: the majority of humans desire to return to the womb through metaphysical means. Are they adults or children? Although I am an atheist, I still long for that heavenly promise I read about in Sunday school. Even now, with a degree and everything, I feel like a child in this frankly frightening world. High school never ends, let's remember. ^_~

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Jun 08, 2009 7:46 pm +1 to what BattleMonkey and oOoOoOo; It was probably about 14 when I started pondering these existential questions and having a crisis of faith. Of course, my situation was entirely different from the children in NGE but I can certainly say that that kind of angst is quite natural. It's true that most kids that age only have an elementary and juvenile emotional understanding of love and sex; yet the thoughts are still there, and I don't see any of the kids in NGE portraying a maturity far beyond their years. Some of this might be due to the difference in Japanese and Western culture. I remember reading that in Japan High School is like what our college is here. Kids are forced to grow up faster in Japan and some cultural critics say that's why adult Japanese spend so much time obsessed with what some would deem childish things; even their technological advancements show a kind of childlike wonder and obsession that we might have as kids but lack the knowledge of actually being able to invent something.

[]: Re: Age and Maturity             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Jun 08, 2009 9:36 pm There is no hard and fast line between mature and immature, it's just a shift of mindset. Many factors can potentially cause a person to start behaving, thinking, and living in a more mature fashion, but nothing guarantees it. Age correlates to maturity only in that the older one is the greater chance that person has had come into the mature mindset for whatever reason... but there are certainly plenty of adults that aren't all that mature. Oh, and by the way, Gamer_2k4 wrote: was jealous of the way he visited the comatose Asuka everyday.

[]: Re: Age and Maturity             Quote Post by Gamer_2k4 » Mon Jun 08, 2009 9:43 pm NemZ wrote: Gamer_2k4 wrote: was jealous of the way he visited the comatose Asuka everyday.

[]: Re: Age and Maturity             Quote Post by NemZ » Mon Jun 08, 2009 10:02 pm Ah, missed that important difference in your post. my bad.

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Mon Jun 08, 2009 10:49 pm oOoOoOo wrote: Personally, I see little connection between age and maturity, once you've hit adolescence. Experience doesn't equal maturity, either. Rather, it is if you learn from your experience. In that sense, it is all about intelligence. The stupidest adult will always be less mature than the smartest child.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Mon Jun 08, 2009 10:53 pm I think you give children too little credit. Sure, the pilots may not be your typical 14 year olds, but I'm fairly certain that the level of maturity they show in the series at times is surpassed by children who may even be younger than they. And it is only at times too, not constant. That said, due to their times of so called immaturity, I think that if they were replaced by 20 year olds and their behaviors remained the same, the story would seem rather unnatural. oOoOoOo wrote: High school never ends, let's remember. ^_~

[]: Re: Age and Maturity             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Mon Jun 08, 2009 11:01 pm Gamer_2k4 wrote: It's pretty clear that the children in NGE aren't anything close to normal fourteen year olds. Their bodies, thoughts, actions, and emotions aren't at all representative of someone as young as they are. The pilot cast could've been replaced by twenty year olds and it would have felt much more fitting, IMO.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Jun 09, 2009 2:06 am At nearly 40 years remove -- and having done my best to erase all recollection of those years -- I can't really say how at 14 I would have measured against the Children. One thing to bear in mind in all this is the dichotomy between intelligence and wisdom (as a D&D character, Asuka would be your typical INT18 WIS6 magic user). The extreme prolongation of childhood is a very recent (and, IMHO, very unhealthy) phenomenon -- the age at which people could leave school and find work (and many were expected to) has risen from 14 to 18 ( de jure ) or 21+ ( de facto ) in my lifetime; and with it the associated attempts to bubblewrap young people during that time.

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Tue Jun 09, 2009 5:08 am These eva pilots we're talking about and eva pilots in Japan at that. At this age many 14-year olds are perfectly able to articulate some questions of their existence (I know I did and my friends/siblings as well); to say otherwise is a true insult to their intelligence. Kids are forced to grow up faster in Japan

[]:             Quote Post by BattleMonkey » Tue Jun 09, 2009 7:43 am Mr. Tines wrote: At nearly 40 years remove -- and having done my best to erase all recollection of those years -- I can't really say how at 14 I would have measured against the Children. One thing to bear in mind in all this is the dichotomy between intelligence and wisdom (as a D&D character, Asuka would be your typical INT18 WIS6 magic user).

[]:             Quote Post by Alaska Slim » Tue Jun 09, 2009 9:25 am Mr. Tines wrote: The extreme prolongation of childhood is a very recent (and, IMHO, very unhealthy) phenomenon -- the age at which people could leave school and find work.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Tue Jun 09, 2009 12:09 pm Mr. Tines wrote: The extreme prolongation of childhood is a very recent (and, IMHO, very unhealthy) phenomenon -- the age at which people could leave school and find work (and many were expected to) has risen from 14 to 18 ( de jure ) or 21+ ( de facto ) in my lifetime; and with it the associated attempts to bubblewrap young people during that time.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Jun 09, 2009 12:19 pm The Children struck me as pretty much the same as any other teenager (except Rei and Kaworu, obviously) except that they have dead parents/abandonment issues, they LITERALLY HAVE THE PRESSURE OF THE WHOLE WORLD ON THEM, and they've been mindraped by Angels a few times. /thread

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 09, 2009 12:54 pm UrsusArctos wrote: As for the poor Children, well...despite being independent in their own right, they really haven't gained the strength they need to handle their emotional world on their own terms.

[]:             Quote Post by BattleMonkey » Tue Jun 09, 2009 1:44 pm NemZ wrote: UrsusArctos wrote: As for the poor Children, well...despite being independent in their own right, they really haven't gained the strength they need to handle their emotional world on their own terms.

[]:             Quote Post by xtr00kvltcorex » Tue Jun 09, 2009 3:04 pm in addition to already mentioned points, one must also take into account some of the particular experiences they have had. namely Asuka walking in on her mom's suicide and the extreme neglect and ever-changing shift in parental figures that preceded it. Shinji basically witnessed his mother getting "killed", followed subsequently by abandonment by Gendo. Such things could impact one by that age to be not so "teenagey". personally i had a lot of crappy and somewhat similar experiences growing up, with shittier ones that followed after i was 14 and i was more than well aware of what sex was.
